food_subgroup: Tofu and soy-based dishes with sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for sweet and sour crispy tofu. FOODON_03302757 (tofu-based food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Tofu, cooked) is the closest available code. No specific code exists for sweet and sour crispy tofu preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 102263004 identifies tofu as a substance; no specific concept exists for this preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical shared serving of sweet and sour crispy tofu at a Malaysian or Singaporean zi char restaurant is approximately 250 g, intended for sharing among two to three diners.

nutrient_source.title: Tahu goreng (fried tofu) with sweet and sour sauce — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Tofu, raw, firm (NDB 16426) and Sauce, sweet and sour, ready-to-serve (NDB 06164), weighted by recipe proportion
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for sweet and sour crispy tofu exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy components: firm tofu (NDB 16426) at approximately 60% by weight of finished dish, sweet and sour sauce (NDB 06164) at approximately 25%, and frying oil absorption at approximately 15%. Energy adjusted upward to reflect deep-frying oil uptake (~8–10 g fat per 100 g fried tofu). All values are estimates and should be verified against laboratory analysis.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of soybean-based foods and tofu preparations — analogous estimate based on Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sweet and sour crispy tofu. GI estimated at 50 based on: (1) plain tofu GI reported as approximately 15 (very low, soy protein matrix); (2) sweet and sour sauce contribution raises effective GI due to added sugars and ketchup; (3) deep-frying fat content slows gastric emptying, moderating GI; (4) composite GI estimated at 50 (low category, ≤55) using weighted blend of tofu GI ~15 and sweet-sour sauce GI ~65, weighted 60:40 by carbohydrate contribution. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (50 × 14.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 18.1 g. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a 250 g serving contributes approximately 775 mg sodium, representing about 34% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or fewer and request reduced-sodium soy sauce preparation, as the sweet and sour sauce and soy sauce base contribute significant sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugars in sweet and sour sauce (approximately 8.2 g sugar per 100 g) and moderate glycemic index of 50 result in a glycemic load of approximately 18 g per standard 250 g serving, which is in the medium-to-high GL range.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in small portions (100–150 g), pair it with high-fibre vegetables or brown rice to blunt postprandial glucose response, and be aware that the sweet sauce contributes meaningful free sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying adds approximately 9.2 g total fat per 100 g; however, saturated fat is relatively low at 1.4 g per 100 g and cholesterol is negligible, while soy protein in tofu may have a modest LDL-lowering effect.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume this dish occasionally in moderate portions, noting that the soy protein base is cardioprotective but the deep-frying method and sodium content from the sauce warrant portion control and infrequent consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet and Sour Crispy Tofu is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean zi char (Chinese-style hawker stir-fry) dish in which firm tofu is cut into cubes, coated lightly in cornstarch, and deep-fried until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining soft inside. The fried tofu is then tossed in a glossy sweet and sour sauce made from tomato ketchup, rice vinegar, sugar, and soy sauce, typically garnished with sliced onions, bell peppers, and pineapple chunks for colour and acidity contrast. The dish is known locally as 'tauhu masam manis rangup' in Malay and '糖醋脆豆腐' in Chinese. It is a vegetarian and vegan-friendly option commonly ordered as a shared side dish at zi char restaurants and seafood houses across Singapore and Malaysia. Nutritionally, it provides moderate protein from soy (approximately 7.8 g per 100 g), moderate fat from deep-frying (approximately 9.2 g per 100 g), and notable carbohydrates from the sweet sauce (approximately 14.5 g per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 50 (low category), though the added sugar content means glycemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 18 g. Sodium content is moderate at approximately 310 mg per 100 g, primarily from soy sauce. The dish is suitable for vegetarians and vegans and is halal-friendly. Clinically, individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in controlled portions due to its sodium and added sugar content.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for sweet and sour crispy tofu was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for firm tofu and sweet and sour sauce with oil absorption adjustment. GI is an analogous estimate based on published soy food GI data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are approved and the estimation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA SR Legacy (firm tofu NDB 16426 and sweet and sour sauce NDB 06164) with deep-frying oil absorption adjustment; laboratory analysis of the finished dish is recommended for high-confidence values. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero for plant-based deep-fried tofu using standard vegetable oil; no trans fats expected by formulation). 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero as tofu is a plant-derived food containing no dietary cholesterol). 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients). 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero; tofu and vegetable-based sauce contain negligible vitamin D). 6. GI estimated at 50 (low, ≤55) based on analogous soy food data from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); direct GI measurement of this dish is not available. 7. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (50 × 14.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 18.1 g. 8. Sodium estimate of 310 mg per 100 g reflects soy sauce and ketchup contribution; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant preparation. 9. The input food name 'Sweet & Sourcrispy Toufu' has been normalised to canonical English 'Sweet and Sour Crispy Tofu'. 10. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are approximate.
